description: One of the world's great megacities — extraordinary pre-Hispanic history, the most Diego Rivera murals per square kilometre on earth, a culinary scene that runs from $1 tacos to the world's top-50 restaurants, and a creative energy that never sleeps.

neighborhoodRecs: Roma Norte — The most popular neighbourhood for visitors: independent cafes, excellent restaurants, bookshops, safe and walkable.
Condesa — Art deco buildings, tree-lined streets, Parque México, excellent brunch scene, great for couples.
Polanco — Upscale, Mexico's Champs-Élysées (Presidente Masaryk), luxury hotels, best fine dining.
Centro Histórico — UNESCO-listed historic centre, Zócalo square, Diego Rivera murals, Palacio de Bellas Artes, authentic chaos.
areasToAvoid: Tepito and Doctores neighbourhoods — high crime areas, no tourist reason to visit.
Walking alone at night in areas you don't know — Mexico City is large and neighbourhood safety varies enormously.
Flagging down taxis on the street (Radio Taxis only, or Uber/Cabify apps) — 'express kidnappings' from unlicensed cabs still occur.
scamWarnings: Street taxi kidnappings: never hail a taxi from the street. Only use Uber, Cabify, or radio taxis booked by phone or hotel.
ATM at night — use ATMs inside 24-hour OXXO stores or bank lobbies, never standalone ATMs on the street.
Mezcal bar overcharging: some tourist bars in Roma add charges for 'entertainment' not on the menu. Confirm prices before ordering.
Ticket scalpers at Lucha Libre and Palacio de Bellas Artes — buy from official box office only.

airportTransfer.cheapest: Metro Line 5 from Terminal 1 (Terminal Aérea station) to city centre — MXN $5, takes 40–50 min. Very crowded with luggage.
airportTransfer.fastest: Uber from MEX airport — MXN $150–250 ($8–13), takes 20–40 min depending on traffic. Only request from inside the terminal.
airportTransfer.recommended: Uber is the clear recommendation — safe, app-based, no negotiation needed. Mexico City Uber drivers are generally reliable.
airportTransfer.taxiEstimate: Pre-paid taxi from official airport taxi desk: MXN $200–350 depending on zone. Safe and metered, purchased at official desk inside arrivals.

tippingEtiquette: 10–15% at restaurants is standard and expected. Tip in cash even when paying by card.
Tip grocery baggers (usually elderly or young volunteers) MXN $5–10 — they work only for tips.
Uber drivers: tip in-app or small cash tip is appreciated.
Hotel housekeeping: MXN $20–50 per night is the norm.
Lucha Libre beer vendors walk the aisles — tip them MXN $10–20 for the service.

dailyCostBreakdown.accommodation: Hostel dorm MXN $200–400 | Budget hotel MXN $600–1,200 | Boutique hotel MXN $1,500–3,500
dailyCostBreakdown.meals: Street taco MXN $15–25 | Torta lunch MXN $50–80 | Restaurant dinner MXN $200–500 | Pujol tasting menu MXN $2,000+
dailyCostBreakdown.transport: Metro MXN $5 | Uber within city MXN $50–120 | Metrobús MXN $6
dailyCostBreakdown.activities: Anthropology Museum MXN $85 | Lucha Libre MXN $100–200 | Teotihuacán bus + entry MXN $200 | Free: murals, Zócalo

packingTips: Light layers — Mexico City sits at 2,240m altitude. Days are warm (20–24°C) but evenings drop to 10–15°C.
Rain jacket for June–September — afternoon thunderstorms are common and heavy.
Comfortable walking shoes — Roma, Condesa, and Centro are very walkable on flat terrain.
Cash in pesos for tacos and markets — many street vendors don't accept cards.
premium.bookingStrategy: March–May is the best window: low rainfall, warm temperatures, before summer crowds. November (Día de los Muertos) is spectacular but the city fills up. Book Roma/Condesa accommodation 3–4 weeks ahead for weekends.
localTips: Mercado de Medellín in Roma for the freshest produce and tamales — arrive before 9am for the best selection.
Cosme (in NYC) or its CDMX counterpart Pujol for a special dinner — book Pujol 4–6 weeks ahead minimum.
The Anthropology Museum in Chapultepec is one of the world's best — go on Sunday when entry is free.
Michelada culture: a cold beer with lime juice, salt, and hot sauce in a chilled glass is the local hangover cure and afternoon ritual.
Día de los Muertos (Oct 31–Nov 2): Alebrijes parade, Zócalo altar displays, cemetery visits in Mixquic — one of the world's great festivals.
premium.bestTimeToVisit: March–May: warm, dry, clear skies, parks in bloom. October–November for cultural events including Día de los Muertos.
dayTrips: Teotihuacán — 1 hour by bus, the Pyramid of the Sun and Moon are extraordinary at dawn before the crowds arrive.
Xochimilco — 1 hour by metro + tram, floating garden canals, colourful trajineras boats, festive local atmosphere.
Taxco — 3 hours south, silver-mining colonial hill town, extraordinary baroque cathedral, UNESCO-listed city.
visaInfo: Citizens of the US, UK, EU countries, Canada, and Australia: no visa required for tourist stays up to 180 days — passport only needed on arrival
A tourist card (FMM — Forma Migratoria Múltiple) is issued on arrival and must be kept for the duration of your stay and surrendered on departure
180 days is the maximum tourist stay — significantly longer than most countries; extensions are not typically granted for tourism
Mexico does not require a return ticket or proof of onward travel by law, but airlines may ask for it at check-in
Entry into Mexico from the US by land: same rules apply; the FMM is issued at the border crossing
currencyTips: Cards widely accepted in Mexico City's Roma, Condesa, and Polanco neighbourhoods; cash essential for street tacos, metro, and markets (La Merced, Tepito area — cash only)
Best ATMs: Banorte and HSBC Mexico ATMs have lower foreign card fees; Citibanamex (formerly Banamex) also reliable; avoid standalone ATMs in tourist areas
OXXO convenience stores are ubiquitous and accept cards for most transactions — useful for paying phone top-ups and buying transport cards
Always choose MXN at card terminals and ATMs — dynamic currency conversion is aggressively promoted and gives poor rates
premium.simCard: Telcel has the best nationwide 4G coverage in Mexico. AT&T Mexico is a strong urban option. Tourist SIMs are available at Mexico City Benito Juárez Airport (MEX) terminal 2 arrivals. A 30-day Telcel tourist SIM with 30–50 GB costs MXN 200–400. Oxxo stores throughout the city sell and top up Telcel SIMs. eSIM via Airalo is widely available for Mexico.
